author: Corpus Analyst Rowan-C

dimensionSummaries.dimension.player-fantasy: The player is a nimble problem solver who treats walls, height, rented tools, and two related kingdoms as a field of route choices rather than a fixed sequence.
dimensionSummaries.dimension.core-loop: After an opening arc, the player chooses a destination, equips the likely tools, traverses one of two connected worlds, completes a dungeon, and reinvests rewards into ownership or upgrades.
dimensionSummaries.dimension.verbs: Top-down movement and combat are joined by wall merging, lateral movement along surfaces, portal traversal, and a broad tool set regulated by a shared replenishing energy meter.
dimensionSummaries.dimension.world-state: Completed dungeons, rescued figures, purchased tools, discovered rifts, opened shortcuts, and collectible thresholds persistently change access and available strength.
dimensionSummaries.dimension.topology: Hyrule and Lorule form corresponding but nonidentical overworld graphs linked by wall rifts, so a blocked route in one world may be approached through the other.
dimensionSummaries.dimension.gating-revisit: Most major tools become available through rental early enough to support flexible dungeon order, while ownership, wall merging, rifts, and local keys control finer-grained revisits.
dimensionSummaries.dimension.puzzle-information: Wall seams, narrow ledges, visible height, tool-shaped affordances, energy limits, and paired-world correspondence provide the evidence used to form and test traversal hypotheses.
dimensionSummaries.dimension.dungeon-structure: Dungeons remain bounded room graphs with a focused interaction grammar, but the broader set can be attempted in multiple orders because required tools are not tied only to prior dungeon completion.
dimensionSummaries.dimension.exploration-reward: Rifts, caves, minigames, heart pieces, rupees, and hidden collectibles reward both close inspection of familiar terrain and comparison between corresponding world locations.
dimensionSummaries.dimension.growth-economy: Rupees fund temporary rentals and permanent purchases; ownership survives defeat and enables collectible-based upgrades, turning economic commitment into reduced future risk.
dimensionSummaries.dimension.combat-boss: Combat combines positional swordplay, energy-limited tools, wall evasion, and bosses that test the local dungeon rule without requiring a single global acquisition order.
dimensionSummaries.dimension.npc-quest: Ravio converts item access into a shop relationship, while residents, rescued sages, minigame hosts, and collectible-related characters connect optional tasks to growth and world knowledge.
dimensionSummaries.dimension.narrative-performance: The conflict is revealed through transformations, captures, rescues, and crossings between parallel kingdoms; agency resumes between compact scenes so player-chosen dungeon order remains visible.
dimensionSummaries.dimension.camera-blocking: The overhead camera exposes room plans while modeled height and wall-side movement reveal vertical relationships; wall merging temporarily reframes Link as a lateral figure on the boundary.
dimensionSummaries.dimension.teaching-feedback: Flattening animation, energy drain and refill, highlighted cracks, rental loss, purchase permanence, stereo depth, and hint ghosts clearly communicate unusual rules and recovery options.
dimensionSummaries.dimension.ui-input: The interface exposes the shared item-energy meter, equipped tools, map links, and rental status, while optional hint glasses exchange system Play Coins for contextual guidance.
dimensionSummaries.dimension.recovery-accessibility: Defeat returns rented items to the shop but preserves permanent progress; players can re-rent, buy equipment, select another objective, or use optional hints rather than face a hard dead end.
dimensionSummaries.dimension.perspective-implementation: A 2.5D overhead presentation preserves classic planar readability while stereoscopic height, wall planes, and layered floors make vertical separation an actionable rule.
dimensionSummaries.dimension.hardware-multiplayer: The original is a single-player Nintendo 3DS title whose stereoscopic display and Play Coin system affect depth reading and optional hint access without requiring networked play.
dimensionSummaries.dimension.production-runtime: Implementation requires paired-world mappings, wall-surface navigation, reversible avatar projection, a shared energy economy, flexible dungeon dependencies, rentals, persistent ownership, and hint-state checks.

observationThemes.context: A gap, narrow ledge, barred route, enemy attack, or world rift is reachable from a solid wall surface but not from the normal floor plane.
preconditions: A compatible wall is within reach
The energy meter is not empty
playerInput: Exit at a valid floor or enter a rift
before: The obstacle blocks ordinary top-down movement
action: Transform Link into a wall-bound state and traverse the boundary
after: Link reaches a new side, ledge, portal, or safe position
The energy meter reflects the temporary state
feedback: The avatar visibly flattens and the framing emphasizes the wall plane
Lateral motion and energy drain confirm the new movement rule
consequences: Walls become routes rather than only collision
The player can solve traversal, evasion, and portal problems with the same verb
observationThemes.factualDescription: Link can merge into compatible walls, move laterally along them while energy drains, and exit onto reachable ground or pass through designated rifts.
observationThemes.interpretation: A reversible change of movement plane can make a familiar boundary support exploration, puzzle solving, and combat while remaining readable in one camera system.

observationThemes.context: Several dungeons are available and their expected tools can be rented from Ravio rather than acquired only by clearing an earlier dungeon.
preconditions: At least one target dungeon is available
playerInput: Later choose whether to buy the tool permanently
before: The chosen dungeon's affordances cannot be used
action: Spend currency to obtain temporary access and attempt the objective
after: The tool can be used until defeat
Purchase can later convert temporary access into persistent ownership
feedback: The shop and inventory display rental status
Defeat returns rented equipment, while purchase visibly changes permanence
consequences: Dungeon order becomes a player decision
Failure creates an economic retry cost without erasing completed campaign progress
observationThemes.factualDescription: Ravio rents major tools for rupees and retrieves rentals after defeat; the player can later buy tools permanently and use this access to choose among available dungeons.
observationThemes.interpretation: Separating capability access from permanent ownership can support nonlinear objective order while retaining preparation, failure stakes, and a long-term economy.

observationThemes.context: A discovered rift connects a wall position in Hyrule to a related but differently traversable location in Lorule.
preconditions: The destination world contains a useful route or discovery
playerInput: Compare the destination with the corresponding map location
before: The target location is inaccessible or unexplored in the current world
The cross-world connection is not yet part of the player's route model
action: Use the paired spatial link and continue from the alternate topology
after: The avatar occupies the corresponding region of the other world
A route, shortcut, dungeon, or reward becomes reachable
feedback: Landform and landmark similarities support comparison while local differences expose new paths
consequences: Exploration rewards reasoning across two maps
The single-player experience gains systemic depth without depending on online or simultaneous participants
observationThemes.factualDescription: Designated wall rifts connect Hyrule and Lorule at corresponding locations, and differences between the two overworlds allow crossings to bypass barriers or expose discoveries.
observationThemes.interpretation: Paired maps become more than visual variants when each cross-world edge changes route possibility and asks the player to remember both similarity and difference.

observationThemes.context: Within a dungeon room, visible elevation and wall surfaces imply a route, but the player may not yet understand how to combine merge movement with the local tool.
preconditions: Hint glasses may be available as an optional support
playerInput: Test merging or the local tool
Optionally spend a Play Coin on a contextual hint
before: The next floor, switch, or exit is unavailable
action: Apply the inferred wall-and-height rule or request graduated information
after: The room state opens the next dependency
The player retains agency over executing the solution
feedback: Stereoscopic depth, wall motion, switches, and door changes show the causal result
A hint ghost provides information without automatically moving the avatar
consequences: The dungeon grammar is learned for later rooms and its boss
A stuck player can recover without changing the core rule
observationThemes.factualDescription: Dungeon puzzles combine overhead layouts, modeled height, wall merging, and tools; optional hint ghosts can exchange 3DS Play Coins for contextual guidance.
observationThemes.interpretation: Readable spatial evidence plus an opt-in hint economy can preserve discovery for confident players while providing a bounded recovery path for stalled players.

observationThemes.context: Hidden Maiamai are returned to their parent after the player has purchased a rentable tool and reached a collectible threshold.
preconditions: Enough hidden collectibles have been found
The related NPC can be visited
playerInput: Select an eligible owned tool for improvement
before: The tool has its normal combat and puzzle behavior
action: Complete the return transaction and apply the upgrade
after: The NPC acknowledges the recovered group
The selected tool gains a persistent stronger behavior
feedback: Collection totals and the upgrade presentation show the threshold
Subsequent tool use visibly changes its effect
consequences: Optional exploration improves later encounters and bosses
The system must validate both collectible count and permanent ownership before offering the reward
observationThemes.factualDescription: Returning hidden Maiamai at set totals lets their parent upgrade a tool that the player has permanently purchased, changing that tool's later effectiveness.
observationThemes.interpretation: A social collectible turn-in can connect exploration to combat growth while making prior economic commitment a clear prerequisite for the optional reward.

contributionHypotheses: Temporary capability access can support nonlinear objective order when ownership and failure preserve meaningful economic decisions.
A reversible movement-plane transformation can unify traversal, portals, evasion, and spatial puzzles.
Corresponding worlds create strong exploration reasoning when crossings are sparse edges and local topology differs in consequential ways.
uniqueOrNonportable: The names and depictions of Hyrule, Lorule, Ravio, the sages, wall-painting form, Maiamai, exact rift map, narrative scenes, music, and dialogue are nonportable.
Specific dungeon order, item roster, room layouts, collectible locations, boss weaknesses, and visual transformation sequence remain evidence only.
openQuestions: A later accessibility review should test the game's critical depth cues with stereoscopic display disabled rather than inferring equivalence from the product page.
